questions.question: Explain the primary difference between user-level threads and kernel-level threads from a scheduling perspective.
keyPoints: The operating system scheduler is unaware of user-level threads and schedules the process (the kernel-level thread) that contains them.
A blocking system call made by one user-level thread will block all other threads within the same process because the OS has only scheduled the single kernel-level thread.
Kernel-level threads are scheduled directly by the operating system, allowing threads within the same process to run on different processors and for one thread to block without affecting others.

questions.question: Describe the concept of 'priority inversion' in thread scheduling and a common solution to mitigate it.
keyPoints: Priority inversion occurs when a lower-priority thread holds a lock needed by a higher-priority thread, which in turn is blocked by an intermediate medium-priority thread, causing the high-priority thread to wait unnecessarily.
This situation violates the priority-based scheduling policy, as a high-priority task is effectively delayed by a task of lower priority.

A common solution is priority inheritance, where the lower-priority thread holding the lock temporarily inherits the priority of the high-priority thread that is blocked on it, preventing medium-priority threads from preempting it and allowing it to finish and release the lock faster.

questions.question: List two goals a CPU scheduler aims to achieve when scheduling threads.
keyPoints: Maximize CPU utilization by ensuring the CPU is as busy as possible.
Ensure fairness by providing a reasonable share of CPU time to each thread or process.
Minimize turnaround time (the total time from submission to completion) and waiting time (the amount of time a thread spends ready in the queue).
Maximize throughput (the number of threads completed per time unit).
